Chapter 13 • Hard tissue surgery
■  Analgesics (e.g. methadone, buprenorphine) and 
non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). 
NSAIDs are used sparingly because they are known 
to slow wound and bone healing in many species
■  Wound care and bandaging. Liquid dressings are 
commonly used by the authors (e.g. Katuor Film® 
(hexamethyldisiloxane and acrylate copolymer)). 
Rapidly resorbable sutures (Biosyn) are often used 
to close the skin, but a recheck is required 10 to 12 
days postoperatively to monitor the healing of the 
surgical wound
■  A calming environment and exercise restriction 
(limiting the guinea pig’s space to a minimum and 
removing all toys) for 3–8 weeks depending on

or comorbidities.
Antibiotics are not systematically prescribed. The own-
ers and primary-care veterinary surgeon (veterinarian) 
should be aware of potential complications and monitor 
them. Follow-up usually includes visits (typically 1–2 weeks 
after surgery) to assess healing and remove sutures, and 
serial radiographs (3–4 and 6–8 weeks) to assess the 
position of the implants, the alignment and the healing. 
Diagnostic imaging (see above) should be repeated imme-
diately after surgery, 2–3 weeks after the surgery and 
again 4–6 weeks after surgery to monitor the healing 
process and inform the decision of when to remove any 
implants or cast.

Treatment
information about the non-surgical management of mus-
The objectives of fracture management are to allow a quick  culoskeletal disease.
functional recovery, to manipulate the fracture site as little 
as possible in order to permit early healing and to limit 
pain. The principles of fracture management in guinea pigs 
are similar to those established for dogs and cats (Miwa 
and Calvo Carrasco, 2019). However, many constraints are 
specific to guinea pigs: they are sensitive to stress and 
pain, they have limited tolerance to treatment and immo-
bilization, they have brittle bone and they are smaller than 
species with which most veterinary surgeons have more 
experience (Cooper and Schiller, 1976; Miwa and Calvo 
Carrasco, 2019; Pignon, 2021).
Fractures can be treated either conservatively or surgi-
cally depending on many variables. In cases of open 
fracture, initial treatment consists of wound care, bandag-
ing and initiation of a course of an antibiotic with adequate 
bone diffusion, such as azithromycin or chloramphenicol. 
In guinea pigs, few muscle masses surround the distal 
bones. Therefore, it is important to shave the whole limb 
to search for injuries that may have occurred secondary 
to the open fracture (Miwa and Calvo Carrasco, 2019). An Altman tape splint on a 500 g juvenile guinea pig with

(a) (b) (c) (d)
Steps to perform a tie-in procedure for a tibial fracture (see Operative Technique 13.1). (a) The intramedullary pin is placed 
13.3
normograde through the tibial tuberosity. The pin is advanced into the medullary cavity of the distal fragment. (b) Four transcorti-
cal pins are placed laterally, two on each bone fragment (for lighter patients, one pin per fragment may be preferred). (c) The intramedullary 
pin is bent twice with a 90-degree angle so that its free tip is parallel and lateral to the tibia and flush with the tibial tuberosity. The four 
transcortical pins are bent once each with a 90-degree angle so their free tips are lateral and parallel to the tibia and pointing in the 
direction of the fracture site. The bent parts should be long enough to bridge with the pins on the opposite fragment. (d) The transcortical 
pins and the intramedullary pin are fixed in place with a polymethylmethacrylate or epoxy putty.

most commonly used approach for tibial fractures. The  the skin is closed with an interrupted pattern and the pins 
animal is placed in dorsal recumbency, the affected  are fixed in place with a PMMA putty (Figure 13.4).
hindlimb is suspended and the fur is clipped from the 
tibiotarsal joint to the coxofemoral joint.
The skin incision is made craniomedially along the shaft 
of the tibia. This incision starts proximally over the medial 
tibial condyle and curves cranially to the midline of the 
tibia at midshaft. It then curves caudally to end near the 
medial malleolus. This incision may be shortened depend-
ing on surgeon preference and how much of the tibia needs 
to be exposed. The subcutis is incised on the same line. 
The bone is exposed by incision of the crural fascia over 
the medial shaft of the bone. The cranial tibial and medial 
digital flexor muscles can be retracted by incising fascia 
along their borders to free them from the bone.
Pin placement: For a medial diaphyseal oblique fracture, a 
tie-in technique can be used (see Figure 13.3) with the 
intramedullary pin placed normograde through the tibial 
plateau (see Operative Technique 13.1). The technique is 
the same as that used to place an intraosseous catheter 
but with an orthopaedic drill. The tip of the pin is cut to 
avoid penetrating the tibiotarsal joint. Local anaesthesia 
(injection of lidocaine on the tibial plateau) or locoregional  13.4 End of a femoral fracture surgical repair in a guinea pig.

Chapter 13 • Hard tissue surgery
Alternative methods: IM pins alone (Periat et al., 2008) and 
IM pins plus cerclage with 2.0 polydioxanone suture (Aguiar 
et al., 2014) have been successfully used.
Pin care and removal: It is recommended to perform a 
daily lavage and disinfection of the pin insertion sites, to 
administer povidone–iodine gel at the pin–skin junction 
and to keep the guinea pig’s environment clean. Recheck 
radiographs are taken 3 weeks and 6 weeks after the 
surgery to assess the healing process and to decide when 
the external fixator can be removed. When bony callus is 
formed, the external fixator can be removed. The pin inser-
tion wounds should be disinfected with chlorhexidine.

A cutaneous stab incision is made on the medial  A transcortical pin is placed in the proximal  
13.1.5 13.1.6
aspect of the tibia on the proximal fragment. fragment using a drill or a handpiece.
  PRACTICAL TIP
Lancet-tipped pins offer certain advantages over trocar-tipped pins, particularly in terms of bone preservation 
and precision during insertion. The lancet tip provides a more gradual entry into the bone, minimizing the risk of 
cortical bone cracking or splitting, which is especially useful due to the small and fragile bones of guinea pigs. 
The sharper and flatter profile of the lancet tip allows for better directional control, which is beneficial in 
procedures requiring precise alignment. Lancet-tipped pins tend to penetrate bone with less resistance, 
reducing mechanical stress on the bone and the surgical instruments.
5  A cutaneous stab incision is made on the medial aspect of the tibia on the distal fragment (Figure 13.1.7).
6  A transcortical pin is placed in the distal fragment using a drill or a handpiece (Figure 13.1.8).

7  The intramedullary pin is bent twice at a 90-degree angle in order to play the role of a connective bar (Figure 13.1.9).

90-degree angle. pins are bent at 90-degree angles.
10 An epoxy putty is placed around the pins to create two connective bars parallel to the axis of the tibia.
  PRACTICAL TIP
The epoxy putty should be kneaded by the surgeon’s hands until warmth is felt in the fingers, indicating the 
exothermic reaction between the two components. Once mixed thoroughly, the putty is applied around the 
pins. Inadequate mixing, which does not produce the exothermic reaction, may compromise the strength of 
the connecting bar.
Closure
The skin is closed with tissue glue or sutures.
Postoperative care
Analgesic and anti-inflammatory drugs are administered (see Chapter 7 and Appendix 2). The animal should rest in 
a quiet and clean environment to limit exercise and prevent any risk of wound infection at the pin insertion sites. The 
animal should be monitored for complications associated with pain (anorexia) and swelling of the limb.
OPERATIVE TECHNIQUE 13.2 
Hindlimb amputation
Patient preparation
The animal is anaesthetized, taking into account the analgesic protocol with opioids and locoregional analgesia (see 
Chapter 7).
Patient positioning
The patient is positioned in lateral recumbency. The affected hindlimb is clipped circumferentially from the dorsal 
midline to the tarsus. The limb is then suspended, prepared and draped (Figure 13.2.1).

Equipment
In addition to the basic surgical equipment common to all procedures (see Chapter 12), a bipolar electrosurgical unit 
is preferable and an additional scalpel blade should be available.
Surgical technique
Approach
The skin and subcutaneous tissue are incised using a curved incision on the lateral aspect of the limb that begins at 
the flank fold and extends caudodistally to the distal third of the femur then caudodorsally to the tuber ischii (Figure 
13.2.2). A similar incision is made on the medial aspect of the limb, connecting at either edge (Figure 13.2.3).

Surgical manipulations
T T
1  On the medial aspect of the limb, the femoral artery and vein are isolated, ligated and transected within the femoral 
I triangle (Figure 13.2.4). The femoral triangle is an anatomical region located on the medial aspect of the proximal thigh  I
and is an important surgical landmark. The cranial border is the sartorius muscle, the caudal border is the pectineus 
V V
muscle, and the base is the inguinal ligament.
E E
2  The femoral nerve is identified, blocked and sectioned (Figure 13.2.5).

and transected within the femoral triangle. sectioned.
3  The pectineus, sartorius, gracilis and adductor muscles are transected at the level of the midshaft femur. The iliopsoas 
muscle is transected at its insertion on the lesser trochanter and retracted cranially to fully expose the coxofemoral joint 
capsule (Figure 13.2.6).
4  The joint capsule is incised, the round ligament is cut, and the femoral head is exposed and luxated (Figure 13.2.7).
The iliopsoas muscle is transected at its  The joint capsule is incised, the round ligament

5  On the lateral aspect of the limb, the skin and the subcutaneous tissue are retracted to expose the muscle bellies of the 
biceps femoris and the tensor fascia latae. Using a bipolar electrocautery unit, these muscles are transected at the level of 
midshaft femur (Figure 13.2.8).
6  These muscles are reflected proximally to expose the sciatic nerve (Figure 13.2.9). The sciatic nerve is blocked by injecting 
it with lidocaine and bupivacaine prior to transection.

7  Using electrocautery, the gluteal muscle is divided at its insertions on the greater trochanter (Figure 13.2.10). The 
T semitendinosus and semimembranosus muscles are transected at the level of the proximal femur and reflected 
I proximally. The external rotator and quadratus femoris muscles are severed at their insertions on the trochanteric fossa.

13.2.10 13.2.11 The limb is removed.
on the greater trochanter.
Closure
The muscles are inverted and the lateral fascial sheaths of the muscle are closed with interrupted patterns using 
2 metric (3/0 USP) polydioxanone suture. Dead space is eliminated by interrupted patterns in the subcutaneous 
tissue. The skin edges are apposed using intradermal sutures and tissue glue (Figure 13.2.12).
The skin edges are apposed using intradermal  
13.2.12
sutures and tissue glue.
Postoperative care
Analgesic and anti-inflammatory drugs are administered (see Chapter 7 and Appendix 2). The animal should rest in 
a quiet and clean environment. The animal should be monitored for complications associated with pain (anorexia), 
swelling of the wound and auto mutilation of the wound.
  PRACTICAL TIP
To prevent any neuropathic postoperative pain, after blocking the nerve, use a new scalpel blade to cut the 
sciatic and femoral nerves.
